meta.title: Behavioral Health Practice Software | ABA Scheduling, Crisis Flow & RTM | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to behavioral health practice operations. ABA block scheduling. Crisis walk-in flow. Variable session length management. Behavioral health RTM billing.

hero.subhead: Behavioral health runs on variable sessions, mixed modalities, and a patient population that sometimes needs to be seen today. ABA blocks look different from therapy hours. Crisis walk-ins can't wait. clinIQ manages the operational complexity of behavioral health without adding to it.

sections.content:
Behavioral health practices face operational challenges that most practice management software wasn't designed for.

**The scheduling complexity:**

Behavioral health isn't 15-minute or 30-minute slots. Sessions are 45 minutes, 50 minutes, 90 minutes. Groups are 60-90 minutes with 6-12 participants. ABA sessions are 2-4 hours. The scheduling grid doesn't map to standard appointment blocks.

**The ABA complexity:**

Applied Behavior Analysis practices add another layer: RBT schedules, supervision requirements, session notes per behavior technician, and authorization tracking by client for authorized hours.

**The crisis reality:**

Patients in behavioral health sometimes arrive in crisis. They need to be seen — not scheduled for two weeks from now. Practices without crisis walk-in protocols either turn patients away or disrupt the entire day's schedule.

**The privacy requirements:**

Behavioral health has heightened privacy expectations beyond standard HIPAA. Patients in a lobby shouldn't hear other patients' names. Providers shouldn't discuss cases within earshot of others. The check-in and flow experience matters.

ABA practices schedule differently from traditional behavioral health — clients have authorized hours per week, sessions are 2-4+ hours, and multiple RBTs work with multiple clients simultaneously.

**The ABA scheduling challenge:**

Client A has 20 authorized hours/week. Client B has 15. Client C has 10. Each needs their hours spread across the week based on school schedule, parent availability, and RBT assignments.

Each RBT can work with multiple clients in a day but has their own schedule and supervision requirements.

**What clinIQ does:**

Authorized hours tracking: Hours authorized per client, hours scheduled, hours completed, hours remaining. Running out of authorized hours before authorization expires → alert.

RBT scheduling: Each RBT's weekly schedule across clients. Who's assigned where? Who has availability?

Supervision tracking: BCBA supervision requirements per RBT. Supervision sessions scheduled and documented.

Session notes compliance: ABA requires detailed session notes per RBT per session. Missing notes flagged.

**The billing connection:**

ABA bills by the hour (97153, 97155, etc.). Hours billed must match hours documented. Discrepancies between scheduled, completed, and billed create audit risk.

clinIQ tracks this alignment — scheduled hours, completed hours, documented hours, billed hours — surfacing gaps before they become billing problems.

Crisis presentations are a clinical and operational reality in behavioral health. A patient calling in acute distress needs to be seen today — the system needs to support that without breaking everything else.

**The crisis failure mode:**

Without a crisis protocol:
- Receptionist receives distressed call
- Tries to find an opening in schedule
- Nothing available
- Refers patient to ED
- Clinical escalation that wasn't necessary

Or:
- Patient arrives in crisis
- Squeezed into schedule
- Other patients wait
- No documentation of crisis presentation
- Clinical and liability risk

**What clinIQ does:**

Crisis queue: Separate from the regular schedule. Crisis patients tracked independently.

On-call visibility: Which providers have crisis capacity today? Who's on call?

Crisis documentation: Crisis presentation, risk assessment, intervention, and disposition all documented in the crisis workflow — not crammed into a routine note.

Escalation tracking: If crisis patient needs higher level of care (IOP, inpatient), referral and disposition documented.

**The warm handoff:**

For practices that receive crisis referrals from EDs or other providers, warm handoff protocol documented — who referred, what happened before arrival, disposition planned.

Behavioral health sessions vary in length, modality, and provider mix — in ways that don't fit into standard scheduling templates.

**The session complexity:**

Individual therapy: 45-50 minutes or 90 minutes (CPT codes differ)

Couple/family therapy: 50 or 90 minutes (different codes again)

Group therapy: 60-90 minutes, multiple participants billing separately

Psychiatric medication management: 15-30 minutes

Psychological testing: Multi-hour sessions, sometimes across multiple days

ABA: 2-4+ hours

**What clinIQ does:**

Session type templates: Each session type has its default length and associated codes. When a session is scheduled, the right time block is used automatically.

Group enrollment: Group sessions show all enrolled members. Attendance tracked per session. Individual group notes generated.

Time-based code compliance: For time-based codes (90837, 90847, etc.), actual session length documented to support the code billed.

**Provider type tracking:**

Licensed therapists, licensed counselors, social workers, and supervised clinicians have different billing rules. Supervised clinician billing requires supervisor co-signature. clinIQ tracks these requirements.

Between-visit monitoring for behavioral health patients is now billable as RTM — and it provides genuine clinical value.

**What behavioral health patients track:**

Depression and anxiety:
- PHQ-9 / GAD-7 scores
- Daily mood ratings
- Sleep quality
- Activity levels
- Medication compliance

Bipolar disorder:
- Mood stability
- Sleep patterns (sleep disruption is a critical early warning)
- Medication compliance
- Activity and energy levels

SUD / addiction:
- Craving intensity
- Triggers encountered
- Sober days
- Meeting attendance
- Medication compliance (for MAT patients)

Eating disorders:
- Meal compliance
- Compensatory behaviors
- Mood around eating
- Weight trends (in appropriate cases)

**The CPT codes:**

98975: RTM setup. ~$20-25 one-time.
98977: RTM device supply. ~$50-60/month.
98980: RTM treatment management, first 20 min. ~$50-70/month.
98981: Additional increments.

Typical: $120-150/patient/month.

**What clinIQ does for behavioral health RTM:**

Condition-specific templates: Depression patients get PHQ-9 tracking. Bipolar patients get mood and sleep. SUD patients get craving and compliance tracking.

Crisis signals: PHQ-9 score elevates, patient reports passive SI → alert to clinician for same-day outreach.

Pre-session prep: RTM data visible before each session. 'Patient has had 4 nights of poor sleep this week, mood rating declining.' Provider walks in prepared.

Treatment response: Is PHQ-9 improving? Is mood stability increasing? Objective tracking of treatment response.

Behavioral health patients have reasonable expectations of privacy that go beyond basic HIPAA compliance.

**The lobby problem:**

A patient checking in for therapy shouldn't announce their diagnosis or their provider's name in a shared waiting room. Name-calling across a lobby, sign-in sheets, and visible schedules all create privacy concerns.

**What clinIQ does:**

Discreet check-in: Patients check in without verbal announcement. Front desk acknowledges electronically.

Waiting room privacy: No name-calling. Patient receives notification when provider is ready.

Limited information display: What's visible on public-facing displays is limited and appropriate.

**42 CFR Part 2:**

For practices treating substance use disorders, 42 CFR Part 2 imposes requirements stricter than standard HIPAA. clinIQ supports Part 2 documentation requirements including consent for disclosure.

Behavioral health implementation covers session scheduling, ABA workflows (if applicable), crisis flow, and RTM.

**Week 1: Configuration**

Session types configured. ABA client/RBT structure loaded (if applicable). Crisis workflow. RTM templates for active diagnoses.

**Week 2: Training**

Therapists learn RTM and crisis workflow. Front desk learns privacy-forward check-in. ABA team learns authorized hours tracking.

**Week 3: Go-Live**

RTM enrollment beginning. Crisis flow active. ABA scheduling running.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Crisis flow improvement immediate. Privacy improvements at check-in.

Month 3: RTM billing at scale. ABA authorization tracking preventing gaps.

Month 6: Full RTM revenue. Supervision compliance documented.

**ROI Math:**

RTM revenue: 60 patients × $130/month = $7,800/month = $93,600/year

ABA authorization gaps prevented: 2 per month × $500/session × 10 sessions = $10,000/month

**Investment:**

Professional: $499/month

Implementation: $750 one-time

faqs.q: How do you handle ABA authorized hours tracking?
faqs.a: Authorized hours per client tracked against scheduled and completed hours. Alerts when running low before authorization expires. Ensures clients receive their authorized hours without exceeding.
Yes. RTM codes (98975, 98977, 98980) apply to behavioral health conditions. PHQ-9 tracking, mood monitoring, medication compliance qualify.

faqs.q: How does group therapy billing work?
faqs.a: Group sessions show all enrolled participants. Attendance marked per session. Each member billed individually. Session notes generated with group and individual components.
Supervised clinicians tracked separately. Their sessions require supervisor co-signature before billing. Supervision sessions scheduled and documented for licensing compliance.

faqs.q: How do you handle 42 CFR Part 2 requirements?
faqs.a: Part 2 consent tracking for SUD patients. Consent for disclosure documented. More restrictive information sharing rules enforced for covered records.

faqs.q: Can the crisis queue integrate with the regular schedule?
faqs.a: Crisis queue is separate from the regular schedule. When a crisis patient is seen, it's documented as a crisis visit, not overlaid on the regular schedule. Providers with crisis capacity are visible separately.
Teletherapy sessions tracked same as in-person with appropriate modifiers. Place of service and telehealth documentation requirements handled per payer.

testimonial.quote: Our ABA practice had no visibility into authorized hours until they were almost gone. Now we see every client's hours in real time and have never had an authorization gap.
